mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes‑Benz GLS350d / GLS400d engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned GLS diesel engine typically costs £4,500–£7,500 supply only, with the OM656 variant at the higher end. Fitting adds £1,000–£1,500 depending on the specialist. A replacement OM642 engine typically costs between £1,500 and £3,500 for a used unit. A full engine replacement on a Mercedes can range from £9,000 to £13,000. Savings of up to £2,500 versus dealer pricing are available on common OM642 repairs. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your GLS.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes‑Benz GLS350d / GLS400d engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends on which engine your GLS has. The OM642 (X166 GLS350d, 2013–2019) is generally considered reliable when properly maintained, but has well‑documented vulnerabilities: oil cooler seal leaks and timing chain stretch. The OM656 (X167 GLS400d, 2019–present) is a newer, more powerful and refined engine, generally considered reliable, but owners have reported camshaft and rocker wear around 100,000 miles, and emission system issues. With proper maintenance and proactive attention to known issues, both can be reliable.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes‑Benz GLS350d / GLS400d?

acceptedAnswer.text: GLS350d (OM642): Oil cooler seal leaks (oil loss in the V of the engine — labour‑intensive repair), timing chain stretch (rattling on cold start — can destroy the engine if ignored), turbo actuator failure, EGR/DPF clogging, and HPFP failure. GLS400d (OM656): Camshaft and rocker wear (tapping/popping noises around 100,000 miles), turbocharger issues, EGR and DPF clogging, and water pump/vacuum circuit recall issues.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a GLS diesel engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the vehicle value and engine era. Against a well‑kept X167 GLS400d worth £40,000–£60,000+, a £6,000–£8,000 recon is 10–15% of the car's value — worth it. Against a tired X166 GLS350d worth £15,000–£25,000, the same recon may approach our 70% Mercedes‑Benz threshold — get a scrap valuation first. The X167 OM656 is the most desirable due to its straight‑six refinement, increased power and modern emissions tech.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes‑Benz GLS350d / GLS400d engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: X166 GLS350d (2013–2019): OM642. X167 GLS400d (2019–present): OM656. Check the VIN plate or vehicle documentation. The engine code is also stamped on the engine block. The GLS350d produces 258 bhp; the GLS400d produces 330 bhp.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the OM642 oil cooler seals, or do they need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: The oil cooler seals must be replaced — they are a known weakness on the OM642. Mercedes used seals of an inferior material that degrade over time. A replacement costs £1,000–£2,500. The repair is labour‑intensive as many ancillary parts must be removed to access the cooler in the V of the engine. Ignoring oil loss can lead to serious engine damage.

replacementCosts.h2: Mercedes‑Benz GLS350d / GLS400d Engine Replacement Costs
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect current GLS diesel replacement costs. The OM656 variant typically sits at the higher end of the range. See the Era Map above for generation‑specific pricing.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a GLS diesel engine typically takes 14–18 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer). The OM642 oil cooler seal replacement is particularly labour‑intensive as many ancillary parts must be removed to access the cooler in the V of the engine.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: GLS350d / GLS400d — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Essential maintenance — catch before catastrophic failure
Piston‑valve contact means little is left to repair
Essential maintenance — catch before catastrophic failure

buyingChecks.title: Buying This GLS Diesel? Check These First
buyIf: * X167 (2019+) with the OM656 straight‑six (more powerful and refined)
* OM642 (X166) with oil cooler seals replaced with updated purple seals
* OM642 (X166) with documented timing chain inspection or replacement
* OM656 (X167) with camshaft and rocker condition verified
* Full service history with regular oil changes — both engines are sensitive to oil quality
* No cold‑start rattling (timing chain health — OM642)
* No tapping or popping noises (camshaft/rocker health — OM656)
* No oil leaks around the V of the engine (oil cooler seal health — OM642)
* Evidence of regular longer runs (DPF health — both engines)
avoidIf: * OM642 with cold‑start rattle and no chain history — assume it's overdue
* OM642 with oil leaks in the V of the engine — oil cooler seals may be failing
* OM656 with tapping or popping noises — camshaft/rocker wear may be developing
* No service history — both engines are unforgiving of neglect
* Short‑journey ownership history — DPF and EGR issues likely
* Engine warning light with no diagnosis — may indicate timing, turbo, emission or sensor problems

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The GLS350d and GLS400d are Mercedes‑Benz's flagship three‑row SUVs — offering space, luxury and powerful diesel engines. The X166 GLS350d (2013–2019) uses the OM642 3.0‑litre V6 — a widely used and generally reliable engine when properly maintained. However, it has well‑documented vulnerabilities: oil cooler seal leaks (labour‑intensive to repair) and timing chain stretch. The X167 GLS400d (2019–present) uses the OM656 3.0‑litre straight‑six — a more powerful and refined engine producing 330 bhp and 700 Nm torque. The OM656 is generally considered reliable, but owners have reported camshaft and rocker wear around 100,000 miles, and emission system issues. With typical GLS values ranging from £25,000 (X166) to £60,000+ (X167), an engine replacement is a major financial commitment — a well‑maintained X167 OM656 with full service history is the safest choice, but a well‑maintained X166 with documented oil cooler seal and timing chain history can also serve well.

cta.label: → Compare GLS350d / GLS400d engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes‑Benz GLS350d / GLS400d (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for rattling or clattering (timing chain warning — OM642) — see the map above
* Tapping/Popping Noises — Listen for tapping or popping (camshaft/rocker wear — OM656)
* Oil Leaks — Check around the V of the engine for oil cooler seal leaks (OM642)
* Service History — Including which engine era (OM642 or OM656) and oil‑change discipline
* Timing Chain — For OM642 (X166): check for any chain work or cold‑start rattle
* EGR System — Check for any loss of power or warning lights; EGR clogging is common
* DPF Regeneration — Check history; short journeys accelerate wear on all diesel eras
* AdBlue System — Check for warning lights or AdBlue faults (both engines)
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes read; unresolved issues may indicate timing, turbo, emission or sensor problems
* MOT History — Check for emissions‑related failures
